title: The Devanagari Script
explanation: Hindi is written in Devanagari (देवनागरी), a phonetic alphabet where each character represents a syllable. Unlike English, Hindi is spelled almost exactly as it is pronounced — once you learn the sounds, you can read anything. There are 11 vowels and 36 consonants. Each consonant has an inherent 'a' sound; vowel marks (matras) modify this sound.

exercises.question: Hindi is written in which script?
Is Hindi spelling phonetic (written as it sounds)?
What does each Devanagari consonant have by default?

title: Sentence Structure: Subject–Object–Verb
explanation: The most important grammar rule in Hindi: the verb goes at the END of the sentence. In English we say 'I eat rice' (S-V-O), but Hindi says 'I rice eat' (S-O-V). This applies to all basic sentences. Once you get used to this, Hindi sentences become very predictable.

examples.english: She book reads. (She reads a book.)
We Delhi go. (We go to Delhi.)

exercises.question: In Hindi, where does the verb go in a basic sentence?
Which of these is correct Hindi word order for 'I eat bread'?
What does S-O-V stand for?

title: Gender: Masculine and Feminine
explanation: Every noun in Hindi is either masculine (पुल्लिंग) or feminine (स्त्रीलिंग). This affects adjectives, verbs and possessives. Key patterns: nouns ending in -aa (आ) are usually masculine; nouns ending in -ii (ई or ि) are usually feminine. Adjectives change their ending to match the noun's gender.

exercises.question: Nouns ending in -aa (आ) in Hindi are usually…
How do you say 'big book' — किताब (kitaab) is feminine?
What changes in a Hindi sentence when the noun is feminine?
options: Adjectives and verb endings also change
exercises.answer: Adjectives and verb endings also change

title: To Be: होना (honā)
explanation: The verb होना (to be) is essential for introducing yourself and describing things. In the present tense it has several forms depending on the subject. Unlike English, Hindi doesn't use 'a/an' articles — you simply say 'I teacher' → 'I am a teacher'.

exercises.question: How do you say 'I am' in Hindi?
Which form of होना is used with आप (formal you)?
How do you say 'She is a teacher'? (teacher = अध्यापिका)

title: Simple Present Tense
explanation: For regular actions ('I eat', 'She reads') Hindi uses the habitual present. The verb stem takes a suffix that agrees with the subject's gender and number: -taa for masculine singular, -tii for feminine singular, -te for masculine plural. Then the appropriate form of होना (है/हो/हूँ/हैं) is added at the end.

exercises.question: For a male subject in singular present tense, which suffix does the verb take?
How does a female speaker say 'I go'?
What is added after the -taa/-tii/-te suffix in the present tense?

title: The Verb करना (karnā) — To Do
explanation: करना (to do) is one of the most versatile verbs in Hindi. It is used alone ('I do work') and also to form compound verbs with nouns (काम करना = to work; पसंद करना = to like; शुरू करना = to start). Mastering करना unlocks dozens of useful phrases.

examples.hindi: क्या आप हिंदी पसंद करते हैं?
examples.transliteration: Kyaa aap Hindii pasand karte hain?

exercises.question: What does करना mean?
How do you say 'I work'? (काम = work)
पसंद करना means…

title: Postpositions: में, पर, को, से, का/की/के
explanation: Hindi uses postpositions — they come AFTER the noun, unlike English prepositions that come before. The most common are: में (in/into), पर (on/at), को (to/for, marks direct objects), से (from/with/by), का/की/के (of, possessive — changes form to match the following noun's gender/number).

examples.hindi: मेरा घर / मेरी किताब / मेरे दोस्त
examples.transliteration: meraa ghar / merii kitaab / mere dost
examples.english: my house (m) / my book (f) / my friends (pl)

exercises.question: Which postposition means 'in' or 'inside'?
How do you say 'on the chair'? (कुर्सी = chair)
The possessive postposition का/की/के changes form based on…
options: The following noun's gender and number
exercises.answer: The following noun's gender and number

exercises.question: Which postposition marks the source or means (from / by / with)?

title: Question Words
explanation: Hindi question words are straightforward. In yes/no questions, add क्या (kyaa) at the very start of the sentence and raise your intonation. For other questions, place the question word where the unknown information would go. The verb still goes at the end.

exercises.question: How do you turn a statement into a yes/no question in Hindi?
Which word means 'where'?
How do you ask 'Why do you eat?' ?

title: Negation: नहीं and मत
explanation: Hindi uses नहीं (nahin) to negate statements and मत (mat) to negate commands. नहीं is placed directly before the main verb (or before हूँ/है/हैं in 'to be' sentences). मत is used for soft negative commands.

exercises.question: Which word is used to negate a statement?
Where is नहीं placed in a sentence?
मत is used for…

title: Simple Past Tense
explanation: For completed actions in the past, Hindi uses the simple past. The verb stem takes -aa/-ii/-e endings (depending on gender/number). Importantly, for transitive verbs (verbs that take a direct object), the subject takes the postposition ने (ne) and the verb agrees with the object instead of the subject.

examples.english: I ate food. (ने construction, verb agrees with खाना m)
He/She read a book. (verb agrees with किताब f)

exercises.question: For intransitive verbs in the past, the verb ending agrees with…
Which postposition marks the subject of a transitive verb in the past tense?
How do you say 'She went'?

title: Simple Future Tense
explanation: To talk about the future, add the suffix -egaa/-egii/-enge/-egii (for different persons and genders) directly to the verb stem. There is no separate होना needed. Note: for 'I will' the endings are -uungaa (m) / -uungii (f).

examples.english: He will come / She will come.

exercises.question: What is the future suffix for masculine singular 'he will'?
How does a female speaker say 'I will come'?
In the future tense, is a separate होना (hai/hain) needed?
options: No, the ending itself carries the tense
exercises.answer: No, the ending itself carries the tense

title: Numbers in Context & Telling the Time
explanation: Hindi numbers 1–10 are irregular and must be memorised (ek, do, teen, chaar, paanch, chhah, saat, aaTh, nau, das). For telling time, use बजे (baje) for 'o'clock', and आधा (half past) or पंद्रह मिनट (15 minutes). Hindi uses 24-hour or AM/PM context naturally.

exercises.question: What word means 'o'clock' in Hindi?
How do you ask 'What time is it?'
साढ़े पाँच बजे means…
